## Empirical CDF

:::{#def-empirical-cdf}
#### Empirical CDF

For observed values $x_1,\ldots,x_n$,
the empirical CDF is
$$\hat F(t)=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^n I(x_j\le t), \quad t\in\mathbb{R}.$$
Here $I(A)$ is the indicator function:
$I(A)=1$ if $A$ holds,
and $I(A)=0$ otherwise.
:::

:::{#def-order-statistics}
#### Order statistics

For observed values $x_1,\ldots,x_n$,
the order statistics are the sorted values
$$x_{(1)}\le x_{(2)}\le\cdots\le x_{(n)}.$$
:::

:::{#exm-order-statistics}
#### Numerical example: order statistics

If the observed values are $4,1,7,3$,
then the order statistics are
$x_{(1)}=1$, $x_{(2)}=3$, $x_{(3)}=4$, and $x_{(4)}=7$.
:::

:::{#def-sample-quantile}
#### Sample quantile

Given [empirical CDF](#def-empirical-cdf) $\hat F$,
the sample quantile function
(the generalized inverse of $\hat F$)
is
$$\hat Q(p)=\inf\{t:\hat F(t)\ge p\}, \quad 0<p\le 1.$$
:::

::: {#thm-sample-quantile-order-statistics}
#### Order-statistics form of the sample quantile

If $x_{(1)}\le\cdots\le x_{(n)}$ are the order statistics,
then $\hat Q(p)=x_{(i)}$ for $p\in((i-1)/n,i/n]$.
In particular,
$\hat Q(i/n)=x_{(i)}$.
:::

:::{#exm-sample-quantile}
#### Numerical example: sample quantiles

Using the same data $4,1,7,3$,
with order statistics $1,3,4,7$,
the sample quantile function is stepwise:
$\hat Q(p)=1$ for $0<p\le 1/4$,
$\hat Q(p)=3$ for $1/4<p\le 1/2$,
$\hat Q(p)=4$ for $1/2<p\le 3/4$,
and $\hat Q(p)=7$ for $3/4<p\le 1$.
So $\hat Q(0.5)=3$ and $\hat Q(0.9)=7$.
:::

```{r}
#| label: fig-ecdf-sample-quantile-example
#| fig-cap: >
#|   Relationship between the empirical CDF and sample quantiles
#|   for data 4, 1, 7, 3.
#|   (Order statistics are 1, 3, 4, 7.)
#|   Left:
#|   ECDF with points $(x_{(i)}, i/n)$.
#|   Right:
#|   sample quantile function with points $(i/n, x_{(i)})$.
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4
x <- c(4, 1, 7, 3)
n <- length(x)
x_ord <- sort(x)
p_ord <- seq_len(n) / n

old_par <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))
on.exit(par(old_par), add = TRUE)
plot(
  ecdf(x),
  verticals = TRUE,
  do.points = FALSE,
  xlab = "t",
  ylab = expression(hat(plain("F"))(t)),
  main = "Empirical CDF"
)
points(x_ord, p_ord, pch = 19, col = "blue")
abline(h = p_ord, lty = 3, col = "gray70")

p_grid <- seq(0.001, 1, length.out = 1000)
q_vals <- quantile(x, probs = p_grid, type = 1, names = FALSE)
plot(
  p_grid,
  q_vals,
  type = "s",
  xlab = "p",
  ylab = expression(hat(Q)(p)),
  main = "Sample quantile function",
  ylim = range(x_ord)
)
points(p_ord, x_ord, pch = 19, col = "blue")
abline(v = p_ord, lty = 3, col = "gray70")
```
